original_hypothesis.title: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() can be abused to force extremely high gas usage (quadratic scanning) in on-chain calls, potentially breaking integrators that call it in transactions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() scans all pairs in both the basic factory and Algebra factory, then performs nested loops over (totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs)^2 to search 2-hop and 3-hop paths, with additional quoter calls in-loop. If any on-chain integration (or keeper) calls this function in a transaction, it can easily exceed block gas limits and revert. An attacker can further increase cost by ensuring factories have many pairs (permissionless pair creation in PairFactory) so the scan becomes prohibitively expensive.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function is non-view (no view modifier) and uses try/catch external calls inside loops. Even if intended for off-chain use, it is callable on-chain and could be mistakenly integrated. Pair creation is permissionless, so pair count growth is attacker-influenceable over time.
deployment_assumptions: Some production system calls BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() on-chain (tx) rather than via eth_call
PairFactory.createPair remains permissionless allowing an attacker to inflate totBasicPairs; Algebra factory may also grow
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut is declared `external returns` (not view), signaling it may be used in tx flows; no explicit guards prevent on-chain usage.

inventory_refs.function: getAmountOut(uint amountIn, address tokenIn, address tokenOut, address _userAddress)

proof_of_signal.pattern: multiple full scans of factories + nested i/j loops over pairs; quoter calls inside loops; function is not view

preconditions: Factories have moderate-to-large number of pairs (hundreds+)
A transaction context calls getAmountOut() (not an eth_call)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unbounded iteration over user-independent global state (pair registries)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: gas blowup grows with number of pairs; attacker can bloat pair count permissionlessly
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No max hops/pairs scanned per call; no pagination; no view modifier to discourage tx usage

analysis_questions: Is getAmountOut() ever called by other contracts in a state-changing manner (routers, genesis, auctions)?
What are the expected pair counts on mainnet and growth assumptions?
Should this function be view and/or restricted to off-chain usage patterns?
validation_requirements: Search for on-chain callers of BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut
Benchmark gas for various pair counts on a fork
Assess pair-creation spam feasibility/cost on Avalanche

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates many dust pairs via PairFactory.createPair, inflating `allPairsLength`. If any contract calls BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() in a tx (e.g., during a swap route selection), those txs revert due to gas, causing a persistent DoS for that integration.
prerequisites: Attacker willing to pay costs to inflate pair registries

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS of routing-dependent features; indirect user losses via failed tx gas spend
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users of any system relying on this on-chain route computation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium; mitigated by switching to off-chain routing or adding bounds

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No gas-bound tests for route discovery under large pair registries

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: yes; measure worst-case gas and confirm quadratic growth
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: cost to spam-create pairs for reproduction
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor pair count growth and failed txs calling getAmountOut()

validation_result.mechanism: unbounded quadratic iteration with external calls in non-view function
validation_result.impact_summary: BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() performs O(N^2) nested loops over all pairs with external calls in each iteration, where N can be inflated via permissionless pair creation, causing gas exhaustion for any on-chain caller.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The getAmountOut function in BlackholePairAPIV2.sol contains three scanning phases: (1) a linear scan of concentrated pairs for direct matches, (2) a nested O(N^2) loop where N = totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs searching for 2-hop and 3-hop routes, and (3) within each inner iteration, multiple external calls to factories, quoter, and router. The function is declared as `external returns` without the `view` modifier, meaning it can be invoked in transaction contexts. PairFactory.createPair() is permissionless (no access control), allowing any attacker to inflate the pair count. Each iteration of the inner loop makes calls to pairFactory.allPairs(), algebraFactory.allPairs(), algebraPoolAPIStorage.pairToDeployer(), IAlgebraPool.token0()/token1(), IPair.token0()/token1(), and potentially quoterV2.quoteExactInputSingle() via try/catch. With even moderate pair counts (e.g., 200 basic + 50 concentrated = 250 total), the nested loop performs 250*250 = 62,500 iterations, each with multiple external calls consuming significant gas. This would far exceed block gas limits on Avalanche C-Chain.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The nested loop structure is clearly present in getAmountOut(). The outer loop `for(i = 0; i < totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs; i++)` contains an inner loop `for(j = 0; j < totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs; j++)`. Within these loops, external calls to factories, pair contracts, and the quoter are made. The function lacks `view` modifier, pagination, or any bound on pairs scanned. PairFactory.createPair() has no access control beyond basic token validation.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: O(N^2) nested loops where N = totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs, each iteration performing multiple external calls. No bounds, no pagination, and N is inflatable via permissionless pair creation.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: function getAmountOut(uint amountIn, address tokenIn, address tokenOut, address _userAddress) external returns (swapRoute memory swapRoutes){
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Function is not marked as `view`, indicating it may be intended for or usable in transaction (state-changing) contexts. This is because it calls quoterV2.quoteExactInputSingle which is not a pure view function.

Permissionless pair creation allows anyone to inflate allPairs.length, directly increasing the quadratic cost of getAmountOut(). An attacker can deploy many token contracts and create pairs between them.

calling_sequence: 1. getAmountOut called externally (not view, can be in tx)
2. Reads totBasicPairs = pairFactory.allPairsLength() and totConcentratedPairs = algebraFactory.allPairsLength()
3. Linear scan of concentrated pairs for direct match (O(totConcentratedPairs))
4. Nested loop: for i in 0..N, for j in 0..N where N = totBasicPairs + totConcentratedPairs
5. Each inner iteration: pairFactory.allPairs(i/j), algebraFactory.allPairs(), IPair.token0()/token1(), IAlgebraPool.token0()/token1()
6. For matching pairs: _getSwapRoutesFromTwoHop -> _getAmountViaHopping -> _getAmountOut -> quoterV2.quoteExactInputSingle (try/catch) or routerV2.getPoolAmountOut
8. Total gas: O(N^2) * (multiple external calls per iteration)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point calls getAmountOut which computes totBasicPairs and totConcentratedPairs, then enters nested O(N^2) loops. In each iteration of the inner loop, it makes 3-6 external calls for pair/token lookups, plus potential quoter calls for CL pools. The _getAmountViaHopping helper makes additional external calls to _getAmountOut which either calls routerV2.getPoolAmountOut (for basic pools) or quoterV2.quoteExactInputSingle via try/catch (for CL pools). There are no bounds on pairs scanned, no early termination limits, and no pagination.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Some production system calls BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() on-chain (tx) rather than via eth_call
PairFactory.createPair remains permissionless allowing an attacker to inflate totBasicPairs
config_validation.deployment_evidence: deploy-pair-api.js deploys BlackholePairAPIV2 as a proxy with initialize(). No deployment scripts show any on-chain contract calling getAmountOut(). PairFactory is deployed without access control on createPair(). The function's non-view nature is necessitated by quoter calls.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker deploys many cheap ERC20 token contracts and calls PairFactory.createPair() for various combinations. Each pair creation is permissionless and only requires the tokens to be distinct non-zero addresses with no pre-existing pair. After creating hundreds of pairs, any transaction that calls BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut() will iterate through (basicPairs + concentratedPairs)^2 iterations with external calls, easily exceeding the block gas limit (8M on Avalanche C-Chain). Even without malicious inflation, organic growth of pairs over time will progressively worsen gas costs. The function's try/catch pattern for quoter calls also adds gas overhead per iteration.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The code defect is confirmed: getAmountOut has unbounded O(N^2) complexity with N controllable by permissionless pair creation. If any on-chain system (keeper, router, auction contract) calls this function in a transaction, it will fail once pair count grows sufficiently. Even as an off-chain helper, the non-view nature prevents standard eth_call optimization in some RPC implementations. The defect is a genuine design flaw regardless of current integration patterns - the function will become unusable as the protocol grows organically.
recommended_mitigations: Add pagination parameters (startIndex, maxPairs) to limit the number of pairs scanned per call
Implement a whitelist of 'important' intermediate tokens for multi-hop routing instead of scanning all pairs
Cap the maximum number of pairs scanned (e.g., MAX_PAIRS constant already defined as 1000 but not used in getAmountOut)
Consider splitting the route discovery into separate functions: direct routes, 2-hop routes, 3-hop routes with individual pair limits
Move route computation entirely off-chain and only use on-chain functions for verification/execution
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Fork Avalanche mainnet or deploy to testnet. 2. Deploy PairFactory, PairGenerator, and BlackholePairAPIV2 with mock algebra factory. 3. Create 100+ pairs via PairFactory.createPair with dummy tokens. 4. Call getAmountOut in a transaction and measure gas consumption. 5. Incrementally increase pair count and plot gas growth to confirm quadratic scaling. 6. Verify that at ~200-300 total pairs, gas exceeds block limit.
uncertainties: Exact pair count threshold where gas exceeds block limits depends on Avalanche C-Chain gas limit (currently 15M for contract calls) and per-call gas costs of factory/quoter
Whether any production system actually calls getAmountOut on-chain vs via eth_call
The Algebra quoter's quoteExactInputSingle gas cost varies by pool state
additional_testing_needed: Gas benchmarking with varying pair counts (50, 100, 200, 500) on Avalanche fork
Verify cost of creating spam pairs (token deployment + createPair gas) to assess attack feasibility
Search for any on-chain callers of BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut in production deployment
Measure gas difference between basic pair iterations vs concentrated pair iterations in the loop
